name: Almoguera and Sierra de Altomira Loop
name_original: Del Castillo de Almoguera por la Sierra de Altomira — circular desde Zorita de los Canes
description: This difficult 49.8-mile (80.1 km) road cycling route takes you through the Sierra de Altomira, passing Almoguera Castle and offering divers
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Almonacid de Zorita traverse a diverse landscape shaped by the Tagus River valley and its reservoirs, Bolarque and Buendía. The terrain features a mix of agricultural fields, extensive olive groves, and the more rugged Sierra de Altomira to the east. This region, part of La Alcarria, offers quiet roads with varied elevation, from gentle slopes to challenging climbs. Cyclists can expect low traffic and scenic views throughout the area. Best road cycling routes around Almonacid de Zorita The most popular road cycling route is Almoguera and Sierra de Altomira Loop, a 49.8 miles (80.1 km) trail that takes 3 hours 46 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores the foothills of the Sierra de Altomira. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Entrepeñas Reservoir and Casasana Port Loop from Buendía, a difficult 53.6 miles (86.2 km) path. This route offers extensive views of the Entrepeñas Reservoir and surrounding natural features. Local road cyclists also love the Bolarque Dam loop from Almonacid de Zorita, a 39.6 miles (63.8 km) trail leading through the Tagus River valley and past the Bolarque Dam, often completed in about 2 hours 49 minutes. Road cycling around Almonacid de Zorita is defined by river valleys, large reservoirs, and agricultural plains. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to more demanding climbs. The routes in Almonacid de Zorita are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 4 reviews. More than 130 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Almonacid de Zorita's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Almonacid de Zorita?
A: There are over 15 road cycling routes around Almonacid de Zorita, offering a variety of options for different skill levels and preferences. The komoot community has explored more than 130 of these routes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Almonacid de Zorita?
A: The terrain around Almonacid de Zorita is quite diverse. You'll find routes traversing the picturesque Tagus River valley, agricultural fields, and extensive olive groves. To the east, the Sierra de Altomira offers more rugged and challenging sections. The region, part of La Alcarria, is known for its quiet roads and varied elevation, from gentle slopes to demanding climbs.

Q: Are there road cycling routes that explore the reservoirs?
A: Yes, many routes feature the stunning reservoirs of Bolarque and Buendía. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14890765" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bolarque Dam loop from Almonacid de Zorita&lt;/a&gt; takes you through the Tagus River valley and past the Bolarque Dam. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1110608279/entrepenas-reservoir-and-casasana-port-loop-from-buendia" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Entrepeñas Reservoir and Casasana Port Loop from Buendía&lt;/a&gt;, which offers extensive views of the Entrepeñas Reservoir.

Q: What is the difficulty level of road cycling routes in Almonacid de Zorita?
A: The routes cater to various fitness levels. You'll find a mix of moderate and difficult routes. While there are no easy routes listed in the top selection, many routes feature short climbs of about 3 km that are not excessively hard, making them suitable for a range of cyclists looking for a good challenge.

Q: What scenic landmarks or attractions can I see along the routes?
A: Road cycling routes in this region often pass by significant natural and historical sites. You might encounter the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3906128" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Buendía Dam&lt;/a&gt;, the intriguing &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3905216" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Route of the Faces&lt;/a&gt;, or the historical &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3527044" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zorita de los Canes Castle&lt;/a&gt;. The town of Almonacid de Zorita itself boasts medieval heritage, including the Church of Santo Domingo de Silos and the 16th-century Clock Tower.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes in the area are designed as loops. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1014687264/almoguera-and-sierra-de-altomira-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almoguera and Sierra de Altomira Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14890765" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bolarque Dam loop from Almonacid de Zorita&lt;/a&gt;, allowing you to start and finish in the same location.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Almonacid de Zorita?
A: The region generally offers pleasant conditions for cycling. Spring and autumn are often ideal, with milder temperatures and beautiful landscapes. Summers can be warm, but the low traffic and scenic views along river valleys and reservoirs make for enjoyable rides. Winters are generally mild, but it's always advisable to check the local weather forecast.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Almonacid de Zorita?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Reviewers often praise the diverse landscapes, including river valleys, large reservoirs, and agricultural plains, as well as the low traffic on the roads, which creates a peaceful and enjoyable cycling environment.

Q: Can I expect challenging climbs on the routes?
A: Yes, for those seeking a challenge, the region offers routes with significant elevation gain. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1110608279/entrepenas-reservoir-and-casasana-port-loop-from-buendia" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Entrepeñas Reservoir and Casasana Port Loop from Buendía&lt;/a&gt; features over 1200 meters of elevation gain, providing a demanding yet rewarding experience.

Q: Are the roads in Almonacid de Zorita generally quiet for cycling?
A: Yes, one of the significant advantages of road cycling in this region is the low traffic. Cyclists can expect to encounter very few cars even on longer rides, contributing to a peaceful and safe cycling experience.

Q: Are there routes that pass through historical towns or villages?
A: Absolutely. The region is rich in history. Many routes will take you through charming villages and past historical sites. Almonacid de Zorita itself is a medieval town with architectural gems like the Church of Santo Domingo de Silos and the Clock Tower, offering cultural stops during your ride.
tips: Nice area with a castle ruin in the town and Visigothic ruins nearby.

The castle of Almoguera dates from the 15th century, although it was built on the ruins of an old Arab fortification. Little remains of this medieval construction, as the current castle is a reconstruction from the end of the 20th century.

Vellisca is the typical La Mancha town of whitewashed houses surrounded by cereal fields. It is located at the foot of the Altomira mountain range and very close to the source of the Riánsares river. Its natural environment is fascinating.
Old Humilladero from the 16th century that was used as the final point of prayer and meditation for the processions of the Via Crucis. It is located near the town of Almonacid de Zorita.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as 'difficult' and requires a very good fitness level. It's an 80.1-kilometer ride with approximately 950 meters of elevation gain, making it challenging for inexperienced cyclists.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route features mostly well-paved surfaces that are generally easy to ride. However, be aware that some segments may be unpaved and difficult, potentially requiring you to push your bike. A robust road bike or a gravel bike is recommended for consistent comfort and navigability.

faqs.question: What historical sites or natural features can I see along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter several points of interest, including the picturesque Almoguera Castle, the Tagus River in Zorita de los Canes, and the settlement of Vellisca. The route also passes by the Humilladero in Almonacid de Zorita. The broader Sierra de Altomira offers impressive landscapes and low traffic density.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Sierra de Altomira region is generally pleasant for cycling during spring and autumn, when temperatures are milder. Summer can be very hot, and winter might bring colder conditions, though the area is known for its diverse landscapes year-round.

faqs.question: Where can I park when starting this route in Zorita de los Canes?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas are not detailed, Zorita de los Canes is the starting point. As a small settlement, you should be able to find suitable parking within or on the outskirts of the village to begin your ride.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route, so while dogs are generally allowed in public areas, it's not typically considered a 'dog-friendly trail' in the same way a hiking path might be. If you plan to bring a dog, ensure they are safely secured in a bike trailer or carrier, and always keep them on a leash when off the bike.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route through the Sierra de Altomira. It traverses public roads and areas.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly overlaps with sections of the Camino Natural del Tajo (13% of the route), the Camino de Santiago en Guadalajara (4%), and the Camino de Altomira (1%).

faqs.question: Are there any amenities like cafes or pubs along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through areas with low population density, so amenities like cafes or pubs might be sparse outside of larger settlements like Zorita de los Canes or Vellisca. It's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks.

faqs.question: What type of bike is best suited for this route?
faqs.answer: While primarily a road cycling route, some unpaved and difficult segments are mentioned. Therefore, a robust road bike or a gravel bike would be a more suitable choice for consistent comfort and navigability across the varied surfaces.

faqs.question: What makes this route unique for road cyclists?
faqs.answer: This route offers a unique blend of historical significance, natural beauty, and a challenging ride. You'll experience the serene landscapes of the Sierra de Altomira with low traffic, passing by landmarks like Almoguera Castle, making it a rewarding adventure for experienced road cyclists.

waypoints.name: Tagus River in Zorita de los Canes
